question_text: Two wires are made of copper and have the same length. Wire X has a cross-sectional area of 2 mm², while Wire Y has a cross-sectional area of 4 mm². Both wires carry the same electric current. Which wire will have a greater potential difference across it, and explain your reasoning using the properties of resistance.

options.label: A
options.text: Wire X will have greater potential difference because its smaller area produces greater resistance

options.label: B
options.text: Wire Y will have greater potential difference because larger area creates more resistance

options.label: C
options.text: Both wires will have the same potential difference since they are made of the same material

options.label: D
options.text: The wire carrying current will have zero potential difference across it

correct_answer: Wire X will have greater potential difference because its smaller area produces greater resistance
explanation: Resistance in a wire depends on three factors: resistivity (ρ), length (L), and cross-sectional area (A), using the formula R = ρL/A. Since both wires are made of copper and have the same length, only the area differs. Wire X has half the area of Wire Y, so it has twice the resistance. Using Ohm's law (V = IR), if the current is the same in both wires but Wire X has double the resistance, then Wire X will have double the potential difference across it.
